narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive dedication in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which stands out above the typical student's 3.21. Your family comes from a middle-income background, and you've indicated plans to attend full-time, which aligns with most applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 49.3% chance that you might fail it—high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3, both at 2nd decile, well below the averages of about 5th or 6th decile for most students, signaling to the model that your early academic performance in those years raised concerns about bar exam readiness. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward success more than any other factor, showing how your college record truly shines. Your school deciles outweighed even your LSAT score of 31—below the usual 36.7—and your family income, both of which had milder positive pulls toward passing. Compared to typical students who get approved, your deciles are notably lower, placing you in the lower tail of the distribution, while your GPA puts you ahead of the curve. This combination made the model cautious, as it prioritizes features like consistent school performance for bar predictions. While this rejection stings, especially given the barriers many Black women like you face in higher education, it's not the end of your path. We encourage you to consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, seeking ways to improve or document stronger recent academic deciles, and exploring programs with bar passage support tailored for diverse students. You have real strengths to build on—keep pushing forward, and many doors will open. Your journey matters, and we're rooting for you.
